summary:
The primary cause for leeway space lies in the disparity in sizes between deciduous canine or molar teeth compared to their respective permanent replacements as they transition into a new set of dentition during childhood development stages.
The main reason behind leeway space formation stems directly from variations observed among different types of temporary versus definitive human dental structures at critical growth phases within an individual’s life cycle. To be more specific, it results primarily because both upper/lower jaw positions accommodate significantly bigger initial sets before making room later through natural attritional processes linked closely tied specifically towards changes occurring post-eruption involving reductions noted particularly amongst maxillary/mandibular second bicuspid regions transitioning downwards toward third molar emergence periods typically seen around adolescence ages when most pronounced shifts occur leading up until full adult occlusion patterns stabilize by late teens early twenties depending upon genetic factors influencing overall craniofacial architecture maturation timelines varying widely even then still considered normal range given wide variability present throughout population samples studied extensively over decades now well documented via extensive research efforts conducted globally spanning multiple disciplines including genetics anthropology orthodontics etc... 

To summarize only the key clinical point related to your query regarding "Leeway Space" :

**Key Clinical Point:** 
Leeway space mainly arises due to the relatively large size of deciduous canines and molars which do not match exactly with subsequent erupting permanent counterparts resulting in necessary spacing allowing proper alignment & eruption pathways forming naturally without obstruction issues arising otherwise potentially causing malocclusions if left unaddressed properly managed interproximally using appropriate therapeutic interventions like spacers retainers aligners braces etc...
